_description: SETTLED official results for England vs Argentina WC2026 semifinal, 2026-07-15. Platform question numbering (Q1-Q15 as shown in the JTC results UI) differs from our internal numbering (files 11-22); both are recorded per row via platform_q and internal_q. Every 'submitted' value was cross-checked against our final pricing (files 16/17/19/20/21) and matches exactly.
note_on_bracket: Argentina advance to the final to face Spain (who beat France in the other semifinal, 2026-07-14). England eliminated.
rbp_sum_check: sum of the 15 per-question RBP figures below = +96.05, reconciles with the official total

questions.method: DERIVED from market SOT lines (lambda 3.870+3.485 -> P>=8 = 0.454); correlation re-measured
questions.note: 2nd-biggest win. The market-derived combined-SOT number sat well below the crowd and was right. The refusal to chase the ~0.55 empirical corpus up (RULE17) was correct.

questions.method: BASE rate (validated modest edge), pooled walk-forward
questions.note: Marginal loss. A stoppage-time goal occurred; both we and crowd were low, crowd fractionally closer. Negligible.

questions.method: n-weighted EB pool of full club career (n=192) + national + WC2026; high-stakes split null
questions.note: Won on the correct side. The deep-dive that pulled Alvarez to ~market (0.58) and declined to push was vindicated; the null high-stakes finding held.
England lost 1-2, did not advance. Small beat-crowd; market was near coin-flip and slightly favored England, crowd similar.

questions.method: DIRECT market, held at 0.29 after penalty deep-research validated it against 0.30 empirical base rate
questions.note: Clean win. The penalty research paid off directly: held at 0.29 (below crowd 0.33), heeded the documented 'we keep being too high on penalties' lesson, no penalty was awarded.

questions.method: high-stakes deep dive (533 StatsBomb + 81 ESPN), n-weighted ~0.46, hand-set 0.50
questions.note: Small loss. Messi did not score. The high-stakes research was DIRECTIONALLY RIGHT (pulled us from 0.65 down), but the market (0.365) and crowd (0.45) were even lower and closer to the NO outcome. RULE17 kept us above market at 0.50; going further toward market would have won here. Directionally correct, insufficiently so.
Small win on a near-coin-flip question, as expected for a low-premium item.
3rd-biggest win. Trusting the liquid market (0.30) over the compressed crowd (0.44) and NOT inflating on reputation delivered a large win. Bellingham did not score or assist.

questions.text: both teams receive at least one card
questions.method: BASE rate, market/crowd-anchored (cards family, no fitted model)
questions.note: Loss. Both teams were carded. We held 0.55 (the expanded-corpus analysis correctly declined to revise it), but the crowd's higher 0.66 was closer. Cards remain a hard, crowd-anchored family; here the crowd's higher read beat our base rate.

questions.method: GLMM/Skellam validated backtest (0.4805) blended toward market -> 0.44
questions.note: Win. Argentina did have more corners. Our validated corners-comparison Skellam model (the one new backtested composition this cycle) called the correct side and beat the crowd.

questions.method: BASE rate, FLAGGED lowest-confidence in files 18/20
questions.note: BIGGEST LOSS, and it was the exact question we flagged as lowest-confidence. A card WAS shown before the first goal; we sat at 0.35, far below the crowd's 0.50, and were wrong. The low-confidence flag was accurate; the lesson is that a noisy base-rate signal sitting FAR from the crowd amplifies downside when wrong -- our own margin-push research says shrink noisy signals toward the crowd, and we did not shrink this one enough.

questions.method: DIRECT market (Anytime mid 0.360, tightest book); RULE17 caution
questions.note: Big win. Kane did not score. Sitting at the tight market (0.36) below the crowd (0.44) and explicitly NOT pushing up on reputation (the Mbappe/RULE17 lesson) paid off cleanly.

questions.method: DIRECT market (O/U9.5 Over mid 0.249, widest book)
questions.note: BIGGEST WIN of the match. This was the single farthest-from-crowd question and the de-shrinkage pressure test (file 20) identified it as the highest-premium item on the slate (+0.94 modelled). Sitting at 0.25 vs a crowd of 0.45 on a NO outcome banked the largest single result. The de-shrink thesis was directly vindicated.
Win. England did not lead at half. Another far-from-crowd DIRECT question where the market number below the compressed crowd was correct.

Loss. Argentina scored exactly 2 (won 2-1). The market underpriced Argentina's scoring at 0.30 and we trusted it; the crowd's higher 0.39 was closer. A case where the liquid market itself was on the wrong side and the crowd read the elimination-stakes scoring better.

summary: England 1-2 Argentina; Argentina advance to the final vs Spain. Final tally +96.05 RBP, 10/15 beat crowd (67%) -- a strong, positive match consistent with the campaign track record. The winning engine was DIRECT-market discipline on far-from-crowd questions: the four biggest wins (10+ corners +34.89, 8+ SOT +32.30, Bellingham +28.32, Kane +18.03) all came from sitting at a liquid market number well below the compressed crowd and refusing to inflate (RULE2/RULE17). The de-shrinkage pressure test's top-premium pick (10+ corners) was the single biggest win, and the penalty deep-research call (hold 0.29) won +11.62 -- both process investments from this cycle paid off directly. The biggest loss (card-before-first-goal, -28.43) was the exact question flagged lowest-confidence: a noisy base rate sitting far from the crowd, which amplified the downside when it resolved against us -- the standing lesson (shrink noisy far-from-crowd signals toward the crowd) applies. Messi (-2.45) was directionally right but stopped short of the market. Cards (-10.88) and Argentina 2+ goals (-17.37) were the other losses, both where the crowd/market's higher scoring/discipline read beat ours.
